narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.70 and an LSAT score of 35, you've put together a thoughtful application for law school, including your plans to attend full-time and your middle-income family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 64.6% chance that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record that stood out as the strongest influences. Most importantly, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st—well below the typical 5.5 average—pushed the model heavily toward predicting bar failure, as it signals lower performance relative to peers at a critical stage. Your LSAT score, while respectable, worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward success, though not enough to offset the other concerns. Your middle family income bracket also nudged the outlook slightly toward failure compared to higher-earning peers who often show stronger outcomes in the data. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having a slightly lower undergrad GPA than the 3.21 average and decile rankings far below norms, which amplified the risk in the model's eyes. These top three factors—your later-year grades, LSAT, and family income—dominated the decision, with Year 3 performance carrying the most weight. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially as a dedicated full-time aspirant from a middle-income background. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above the 36.7 average or strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or a post-bac program. Building a narrative around any upward trends in your record could also help in reapplying to schools with more holistic reviews. With targeted improvements, you can shift this prediction in your favor next time.
